data.title: Nymex Overview: Crude and Product Prices Rise, Still Face Weekly Loss — OPIS
data.description:
Crude-oil and refined product futures contracts were seeing gains but remained on track for week-to-week losses.

The gains Friday come amid some easing of oversupply fears as Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak told reporters that OPEC and its allies could reverse an increase in production planned to begin next month as the cartel unwinds previous production cuts that had been intended to support fuel prices.

Anticipation of the increased output helped send prices lower this week as markets feared global demand didn't warrant the boost in supply. Markets this week have also faced headwinds amid uncertainty over President Trump's moves to impose tariffs on imports from Mexico and Canada and then his decision to pause some of those levies for a month.

U.S. oil prices were rising by more than $1/bbl, with the Nymex April West Texas Intermediate crude contract up 98cts to $67.34/bbl while May prices gained 99cts to $66.99/bbl. The front-month contract is about $2.40 off last Friday's settlement.

May Brent crude was up by $1.13 to $70.59/bbl while June prices rose by $1.05 to $70.03/bbl.

Gasoline futures were leading product prices higher, with the April RBOB contract 1.38cts in the black to $2.115/gal, more than 10cts lower than last Friday. May prices were up by 1.62cts to $2.1242/gal.

The front-month ULSD contract was struggling to hold onto gains, rising 0.66ct to $2.2304/gal while May prices climbed by 1.57cts to $2.1911/gal. ULSD futures are about 9cts/gal lower on the week.

Trump's decision to temporarily suspend tariffs on goods covered by the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement was having an impact on some wholesale fuel prices in the U.S. on Friday. Trump had originally called for a tariff of 10% on Canadian energy and 25% on Mexican energy imports.

In New England, Irving Oil was previously the only company to pass along all of the tariff via price increases of 20-27cts/gal earlier in the week. Thursday night, Irving cut prices by 17-21cts/gal on gasoline, diesel and kerosene. The moves put the Canadian refiner back into the competitive mix after spending a few days with prices that were prohibitive to U.S. buyers.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

  • Reporting by Steve Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com; Editing by Michael Kelly, mkelly@opisnet.com

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1220 ET - The February jobs report supports the view that the U.S. labor market is roughly balanced, Federal Reserve governor Adriana Kugler says. Speaking at a conference in Portugal Friday evening, Kugler notes that at 4.1%, the unemployment rate has stayed in a narrow range since last summer, and that the 151,000 new jobs created in February was not so far from the monthly average of 177,000 over the past six months. Average hourly earnings have climbed by about 4% over the past 12 months: "With strong productivity growth in recent quarters, I do not believe wages are a significant source of inflation pressure," Kugler says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1220 ET - Inflation pressures remain in the economy, and as a result it might be appropriate for the Fed to stay on pause for a while longer, Federal Reserve governor Adriana Kugler says. Speaking at a conference in Portugal, Kugler notes that U.S. inflation expectations have climbed recently, and also observes "considerable uncertainty over the inflationary effects of new policies and policy proposals." "Given the recent increase in inflation expectations and the key inflation categories that have not shown progress toward our 2 percent target, it could be appropriate to continue holding the policy rate at its current level for some time," Kugler says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1122 ET - February payrolls could ease recession fears, but labor data will likely deteriorate in March, "when recent federal government layoffs will be a much larger drag on employment," Capital Economics' Thomas Ryan writes. Nonfarm payrolls were 151,000 last month, up from 125,000 in January but lower than consensus of 170,000. Unemployment increased to 4.1% from 4% and annual average hourly earnings growth slowed slightly to 4% from 4.1%. Ryan says that the U.S. labor market "remains in decent shape and should be able to weather the DOGE-related cull of federal government employees." He thinks Fed cuts this year remain off the table for now. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1102 ET - Supply of new euro-denominated credit is expected to increase to 450 billion euros ($485 billion), surpassing 2024 supply of 442 billion euros, LBBW credit analysts say in a note. Issuance remained high in January and February, reaching 83 billion euro in total, "the third highest volume in history for this period," LBBW says. Demand for new euro credit is strong, prompting companies to issue more bonds, the analysts say. "On average, investors subscribed four times the volume subsequently placed." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1047 ET - In 3Q 2024, only 47% of newly built apartments were rented within three months, tying with 4Q 2023 for the lowest rate since 2012, excluding early pandemic data, Redfin says. With 142,900 new units completed--the most ever--renters have ample choices, slowing absorption. Landlords are cutting prices and offering perks like free parking, though Redfin warns these may fade as apartment construction permits have dropped nearly 10% year over year. Fewer future options could push rents up by next year, despite a current surplus from pandemic-era builds. The rental vacancy rate hit 8.2% in 2024, the highest since early 2021. Median asking rent rose 0.4% to $1,607, still $100 below its peak. Unlike 2023's declines or the pandemic's double-digit spikes, rents are now stable, reflecting balanced supply and demand. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1027 ET - German Additional Tier 1 (AT1) bond spreads are wider than their eurozone peers, offering investors higher returns, ING's Suvi Kosonen says in a note. The wider spreads are due to lower debt ratings and riskier business models for some of the issuers, ING says. AT1 bonds are high-risk debt issued by banks for regulatory capital needs. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1018 ET - Foreign-exchange traders have yet to come to terms that the heightened uncertainty over the Trump administration's tariffs "is still really, really bad for Canada," says Brent Donnelly, head of forex-research firm Spectra Markets. He says upcoming Canadian economic indicators are going to take on paramount importance, as they will provide evidence about how tariff uncertainty is weighing on activity. Some analysts say the lackluster Canada jobs report is a sign of a pullback in hiring. Donnelly says a downturn in Canadian data "will lead to massive CAD selling," adding "the market loves the theme but hasn't been able to hold on because of the unexpected reaction in USDCAD." CAD largely held steady this week relative to USD. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1017 ET - The dollar's downturn is unlikely to be sustained, Monex Europe analyst Nick Rees says in a note. Markets are currently concerned about risks to U.S. economic growth from tariffs following a sharp downturn in sentiment readings, he says. "But this is at odds with the hard data, which is still yet to turn." It also neglects the effect of unrealized tariffs on other economies, he says. Meanwhile, evidence points to rising U.S. inflation that should see the Federal Reserve cut interest rates by less than expected, he says. The dollar should rally in the second quarter with the DXY dollar index set to peak above 108.000, he says. It last trades down 0.5% at 103.541. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1016 ET - Sterling could underperform this month given the risk of a negative market reaction to the March 26 U.K. budget, Monex Europe analyst Nick Rees says in a note. U.K. Treasury chief Rachel Reeves is expected announce billions of pounds of spending cuts to shore up the country's poor public finances. Rising U.K. government bond yields and increased defense commitments mean the budget might not be received favorably by markets, Rees says. Monex expects sterling to fall to $1.26 by April 1 and to $1.23 in three months. "Further out, however, U.K. fundamentals still look solid in our view, and we have upgraded our 12-month projection accordingly, seeing sterling at $1.33 this time next year." Sterling trades up 0.3% at $1.2927. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1005 ET - The Canadian dollar falls against the U.S. dollar as Canada's latest jobs data look comparatively weaker compared to the U.S. nonfarm payrolls report. Canada employment rose just 1,100 in February, much less than the 20,000 expected by analysts in a WSJ survey. In comparison, U.S. nonfarm payrolls rose 151,000 in February, below forecasts of 170,000. While a touch below forecasts, the expansion in U.S. payrolls looks solid, Fitch Ratings economist Brian Coulton says in a note. This "highlights that the negative impact of tariff hikes and policy uncertainty on economic activity at the macro level is not going to be felt for quite a while." USD/CAD rises to 1.4378 after the data from 1.4343 beforehand. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1003 ET - U.S. median asking rent rose 0.4% year-over-year to $1,607 in February, the first increase in six months, according to Redfin. Rents climbed 0.6% month-over-month, but have been stable for 10 months, unlike the pandemic's 18% surge in 2021 and 4% drop in 2023. Supply and demand are now balanced, with new apartments meeting strong demand driven by high homebuying costs. However, slowing construction may push rents up next year, ending perks like free parking. Austin saw the biggest drop in median asking rent, down 9.4% to $1,404, followed by Salt Lake City and Jacksonville, FL., due to robust housing growth. Rents rose most in Cincinnati and Providence, RI. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0949 ET - Hiring stalls in Canada, based on February data, yet BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter believes the underlying details suggest some resilience--at least prior to tariff implementation. The unemployment rate was unchanged at 6.6%, or 5.6% using US Department of Labor methodology. Employment in retailing and wholesaling climbs by over 50,000, which Porter says indicate consumer strength. And wages edged upward. "It's reasonably clear that the job market had been turning the corner in recent months--until the trade war erupted," Porter writes. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1218 ET - Gap's 4Q results proved to be solid, with the company's comparable sales being positive at three out of its four brands, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. The apparel retailer has made steps to improve its position under its management team, the analysts say. However, there is growth/margin risk ahead, the analysts say. "We think there are still risks around Old Navy maintaining its historical growth and margin rates, especially with growing competition and nearing all-time margin highs," the analysts add, noting that they believe trends at Old Navy --which drives 60% of revenue and even more of profit, will be a key focus among investors near term. Shares gain 12%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1027 ET - German Additional Tier 1 (AT1) bond spreads are wider than their eurozone peers, offering investors higher returns, ING's Suvi Kosonen says in a note. The wider spreads are due to lower debt ratings and riskier business models for some of the issuers, ING says. AT1 bonds are high-risk debt issued by banks for regulatory capital needs. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

Supply and demand are now balanced, with new apartments meeting strong demand driven by high homebuying costs. However, slowing construction may push rents up next year, ending perks like free parking. Austin saw the biggest drop in median asking rent, down 9.4% to $1,404, followed by Salt Lake City and Jacksonville, FL., due to robust housing growth. Rents rose most in Cincinnati and Providence, RI. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0855 ET - Gold futures rise as gold exchange-traded funds report inflows in February. Futures are up 0.15% at $2,930.90 a troy ounce. Gold ETFs recorded strong inflows of 100 metric tons in February, the third consecutive month of inflows, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. This ETF demand helped propel gold to a record high of $2,974.0 an ounce in late February, Commerzbank says. The inflows were mainly concentrated in the U.S., where the largest inflow in a single month since July 2020, was recorded. European ETFs, on the other hand, only recorded a small increase in holdings. China saw ETF inflows of around 20 tons, or an increase of around 19%--indicating a growing interest in gold ETFs among Chinese investors. Until now, most Chinese investment demand was dominated by gold bars and coins, Commerzbank adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0420 ET - Just Group's stock remains set for continued growth in the U.K. annuity market helped by its balance sheet, J.P. Morgan Cazenove analysts Farooq Hanif and Bingdi Fan say in a note. Although the analysts don't expect material positive underlying earnings revisions for 2025 as results for 2024 were close to consensus, they do see an upgrade on Solvency II ratio this year given its strong consensus beat of around 10 percentage points, the analysts say. "We also believe consensus may revise its dividend growth expectations up from here given the dividend per share beat," the analysts say. Shares are down 14% at 140.20 pence but are up 58% over the past 12 months. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0207 ET - Malaysian banks are likely to see moderate profit growth in 2025, supported by steady loan expansion and benign provisioning costs, RAM Ratings says in a note. Net interest margins are likely to remain stable as banks manage funding costs with policy rates remaining unchanged, it says. Loan growth is projected at 5.5%, the same pace as 2024, it reckons. While Malaysia's GDP is forecast to grow 4.0%-5.0%, compared with an expansion of 5.1% last year, risks from impending subsidy reforms and global uncertainties may dampen sentiment, it adds. RAM Ratings forecasts banks asset quality to remain strong, with impaired loan ratios at historical lows. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0055 ET - BDO Unibank's earnings growth is likely to be supported by loan volumes, SB Equities' Katrine Eunice Dolatre says in a research report. The Philippine bank's income growth prospects are expected to be primarily be volume-driven, aided by branch expansion--targeting 100-120 new locations this year--and ongoing investments in productivity, the analyst says. The brokerage maintains its 2025-2026 gross loan forecasts for BDO Unibank at roughly 12%, in line with the bank's low-teens growth guidance for 2025. It keeps a buy rating on the stock but lowers the target price to PHP181.20 from PHP186.40 to reflect factors including a higher risk-free rate. Shares are 1.4% higher at PHP157.20. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2112 ET - Singapore banks are likely to face a challenging year ahead, say RHB Research analysts in a report. Earnings could stay flat this year given the high base effects, they write. Banks could post a modest rise in net interest income and a moderation in noninterest income, due to the high base of other noninterest income, they say. However, fee growth should remain healthy, the analysts say. "With operating income growth slowing, we expect [Singapore] banks to keep a tighter rein on cost resulting in flat jaws," they say. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Singapore's banking sector.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

1241 ET - Spanish banks may face some temporary headwinds in the short-term due to competition in lending and deposits, Deutsche Bank says in a research note. This comes after a period of outperformance driven by resilient net interest income, a more stable interest-rate outlook and a more benign environment supporting activity in the country, analyst Alfredo Alonso and associate Atul Hanamante write. "Despite encouraging 4Q24 results and a broadly positive 2025 outlook, the somewhat weaker 1H25 could potentially drive investors to take a more cautious stance," they say. DB cut its ratings on Sabadell and Unicaja to hold from buy and lowers CaixaBank to sell from hold. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Fed's Kugler says rate policy likely on hold for some time
data.description:
U.S. Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kugler said on Friday that rising inflation risks argue for an extended period of steady central bank interest rate policy.

“Given the recent increase in inflation expectations and the key inflation categories that have not shown progress toward our 2% target, it could be appropriate to continue holding the policy rate at its current level for some time,” Kugler said in the text of a speech prepared for delivery before the Conference on Monetary Policy Transmission and the Labor Market held in Lisbon by the Banco de Portugal.

“Going forward, I will continue to closely monitor the effects of policies on the economy, and I will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and any changes in the balance of risks” when thinking about the future direction of monetary policy, she said.

Kugler spoke as Fed officials are about to enter their customary quiet period ahead of their March 18-19 Federal Open Market Committee meeting. There, officials are widely expected to maintain their overnight target rate range that now stands between 4.25% and 4.5%. While some Fed officials have kept the door open to rate cuts later in the year, inflation pressures, which could worsen due to President Donald Trump’s pursuit of trade tariffs, have been casting a cloud over that path.

Kugler, in her remarks, noted that the public is growing more worried about future price pressures.

“Notwithstanding the well-balanced labor market, there are important upside risks to inflation. Some measures of inflation expectations have risen significantly in the past couple of months,” she said.

Kugler also said the U.S. jobs market is now in balance and that wage pressures are not a key driver of inflation. She said, “I am closely monitoring any signs of changes in the labor market so that we can keep it in the good place that it is now while bringing down inflation to our target.”

Coulton says that "the decline in federal employment in February had little impact on payrolls in aggregate." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0845 ET - Oil futures attempt a recovery after President Trump postpones import tariffs on a range of goods from Mexico and Canada for another month, easing one of the bearish factors behind the recent selloff that include OPEC+ plans to start unwinding cuts next month. "While the delay in tariffs has provided a brief sigh of relief, the market is still walking a tightrope between policy uncertainty and oversupply concerns," Rystad Energy's Mukesh Sahdev says in a note. "The tariff threats sent shockwaves through markets, not just because of potential trade disruptions, but also due to fears of retaliatory moves that could ripple through energy supply chains." WTI is up 1.5% at $67.34 a barrel, and Brent is up 1.5% at $70.52 a barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1817 ET - Australia's filling-station operators and supermarkets face earnings headwinds from the availability elsewhere of cheaper illicit tobacco products, according to Jefferies analysts. They write in a note that recent earnings reports from Viva Energy, Ampol, Coles and Woolworths show tobacco sales falling by between 15% and 26%. They point out that, while gross margins on tobacco sales are low, there are little associated costs, so a drop in sales implies material operating deleverage. They think that Viva likely experienced a 12% drag on December-half Ebit from the lower sales. The other three companies have better managed the decline, the analysts add. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1523 ET - Oil futures post minor gains after being buffeted by news on the tariff front--President Trump agrees to put off tariffs on Mexico and Canada for another month--and reports of U.S. plans to tighten enforcement of sanctions against Iranian oil. Benchmark crudes settled at six-month lows yesterday after a string of losses on OPEC+ plans to start unwinding output cuts, tariff uncertainty and a big build in U.S. crude stocks. "While tariff adjustments may provide near-term relief, global demand uncertainties and rising supply levels suggest that crude oil prices could remain under pressure, limiting the potential for a sustained recovery," Exness market strategist Terence Hove says in a note. WTI edges up 0.1% to $66.36 a barrel and Brent rises 0.2% to $69.46 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0716 ET - Cleveland-Cliffs is launching a company-wide "Buy American" incentive for its nearly 30,000 employees. During calendar year 2025, any Cleveland-Cliffs employee who buys or leases a new American-built vehicle with substantial Cliffs' steel content will receive a $1,000 cash bonus in connection with the purchase. Vehicles produced in a plant outside of the U.S., or that use a significant amount of imported flat-rolled steel, will not qualify for the incentive. "We hope that other companies will follow suit and do their part to incentivize consumption of American-made products," says Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs' chairman and CEO. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0131 ET - Vale Indonesia's new nickel-ore sales initiatives are expected to be a key catalyst in 2025, Nomura's Ahmad Maghfur Usman and Verdhana Sekuritas analysts say in a research report. The nickel miner has disclosed improved feasibility for its upstream mine and high-pressure acid leach projects, with a planned gross investment of US$8.6 billion, the analysts note. Ore sales could drive a three-year revenue CAGR of 29%, according to the brokerage's estimates. The company's limonite ore will supply four new HPAL facilities set to come online by late 2026. Nomura upgrades the stock's rating to buy from neutral, maintaining a target price of IDR4,000.00. Shares are 10.6% higher at IDR3,140.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2301 ET - Hyundai Steel may get an earnings boost from China's steel output reduction and stimulus efforts, NH Investment & Securities analysts Rhee Jae-kwang and S.Y. Lee write in a note. The South Korean steelmaker can benefit from a possible steel-sector recovery, as China's economic planning agency earlier this week said it would mandate steel production cuts to restructure the sector, the NH analysts say. The agency has yet to give specific reduction targets, but markets expect China to cut its steel output by 150 million tons by 2030, they note. NH raises its target price for the stock by 25% to KRW40,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 8.0% higher at KRW31,650. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2144 ET - SK IE Technology may report a narrower-than-expected loss this year as it secures more contracts, including a 291.40 billion won deal in February, Nomura analyst Cindy Park writes in a note. The South Korean lithium-battery separator maker's shipments are forecast to rise 97% in 2025 but fall short of helping it break even, Park says. She expects the company's annual net loss at 74 billion won, compared with her earlier projection of 107 billion won, after factoring in new contract wins and still uncertain electric-vehicle demand. Nomura cuts its target price on the stock by 25% to KRW30,000 and keeps a neutral rating. Shares are 0.2% higher at KRW27,100. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2129 ET - Iron ore prices are higher in early Asia trade. China's economic planning agency has said it would mandate steel production cuts, ANZ Research analysts write in a note, adding that no specifics were given on the volume of cuts in the sector. Some state media reports indicate that steel mills in Shandong have been instructed to cut production by around 4 million tons this year, ANZ says. The weak domestic market has also led Chinese steel mills to target the export market, which has weighed on international prices, they say. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange gains 0.1% at CNY773.5/ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

1958 ET - Jefferies adds Barrick Gold to its North American shopping list for copper--an unconventional call, it acknowledges, given Barrick makes most of its money from the gold it mines. Analysts at the bank say they expect Barrick's attributable copper production to double to more than 450,000 metric tons annually and contribute roughly 25% of revenue later this decade. Copper accounts for about 10% of its revenue today. Barrick joins Capstone Copper, Ivanhoe Mines and Hudbay Minerals on Jefferies's list of preferred North American copper miners. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1853 ET [Dow Jones]--Improvements in global softwood pulp market conditions are expected to continue into the second quarter, Canfor Pulp said, "as global supply dynamics adjust to new hardwood capacity and as pulp producer inventories normalize." However, the company said it is facing persistent challenges associated with the availability of economic fiber in British Columbia. "On the demand side, steady Chinese demand is anticipated to absorb these changes in supply," the company added. Canfor Pulp also said it is monitoring the trade situation between Canada and the U.S., with mitigation plans under way to mostly offset the impact of tariffs on U.S. shipments. The company said it is "well-positioned to respond to actual and potential tariffs." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1849 ET - Rio Tinto has no need to raise equity, say Citi analysts Paul McTaggart and Ephrem Ravi. "So let's move on," they say in a client note. Rio Tinto's recent comments about a potential equity raise caught the market off guard and prompted a debate over whether the miner was seeking to rebalance shares in its dual-listed structure, strengthen its balance sheet, or have greater flexibility, say McTaggart and Ravi. "Even under downside scenarios, we think that Rio's balance sheet can handle commodity price volatility without new equity," they say. And, "if management believes Rio's shares are cheap, why dilute shareholders now?" Citi has a neutral rating at A$130 target on Rio Tinto. The stock is up 0.3% in Sydney at A$115.30. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1657 ET - West African Resources' share price may have shot up 19% in two days after its annual result beat expectations, but Barrenjoey says the miner remains its top stock pick among West African gold companies. "One year forward earnings multiples are still eye watering," says analyst Richard Knights. The bank assumes West African Resources offers a free cash flow yield of 32% in 2026. "If Kiaka is delivered in the September quarter of 2025 as expected, we don't believe this discount will persist, even considering the market's concerns around sovereign risks in Burkina Faso," Barrenjoey says. Its price target rises 3.9%, to A$2.65/share. West African Resources ended Thursday at A$2.11. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1337 GMT - MDA Space says that M&A is a big part of the company's expansion plans, opening up new markets and access to new talent. CEO Mike Greenley says on an analyst call that he has specific acquisition strategies: to be more vertically integrated and to secure the supply chain, and to add more capabilities in other geographic areas. The executive says the company is particularly focused on the US and Europe, where there is strong demand from governments for aerospace products. Another part of the consideration is access to talent in new geographies that could support the business. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1333 GMT - MDA Space CEO Mike Greenley says the tariff situation is manageable for its business. On an analyst call, the executive says the aerospace company is actively engaged in dialogues with governments. "Tariffs come and tariffs go," the CEO says, but "when we look at our contracts, and we look at how tariffs work, we don't see a concern for us at this time." Part of the security comes from its customers, where about 90% of its business is from outside the U.S. The CEO also says the company's customers have the same perspective, and he notes that its new business pipeline remains "robust." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1311 GMT - MDA Space says that most of its supply chain is based outside of the US. On an analyst call, executives of the Canadian aerospace company, say that only about one-quarter of its supply chain is sourced in the U.S., while the rest is part of a larger global network. The company is still keeping an eye on the evolving US tariff situation, but says that it hasn't felt a dampening of demand for its products and services. The company's backlog as of the end of the quarter was C$4.39 billion, up from C$3.1 billion a year earlier. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1156 GMT - Canada's auto industry likely won't face a prolonged tariff situation, according to TD Cowen. In a report, analyst Brian Morrison says that the recent implementation then reversal of tariffs shows the complexity of the auto supply chain and how prolonged tariff duration would be negative to the U.S.'s own auto industry and economy. "This is not the intent of the Trump administration, in our view, and as such we anticipate in time to see modest changes favouring a gradual shift to U.S. content with limited disruption by H2/25," he says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1116 GMT - Cleveland-Cliffs is launching a company-wide "Buy American" incentive for its nearly 30,000 employees. During calendar year 2025, any Cleveland-Cliffs employee who buys or leases a new American-built vehicle with substantial Cliffs' steel content will receive a $1,000 cash bonus in connection with the purchase. Vehicles produced in a plant outside of the U.S., or that use a significant amount of imported flat-rolled steel, will not qualify for the incentive. "We hope that other companies will follow suit and do their part to incentivize consumption of American-made products," says Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs' chairman and CEO. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1037 GMT - Deutsche Post's valuation looks fair at current levels, but HSBC cautions that trade headwinds from tariff policies may dent the volume outlook. The company's share price was up 14% on the day of its results, on a higher buyback and better margins in the express business, despite cautious guidance and muted macro and business-to-business recovery, HSBC says. The capital markets day on April 3 is a potential catalyst as the company unveils its growth ambitions, the bank says. HSBC downgrades its rating to hold from buy and lifts its target price to 47 euros from 41 euros. Shares fall 3.9% to 42.55 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0858 GMT - Deutsche Post's guidance reassures as it has been set at a manageable level, Deutsche Bank analyst Andy Chu writes. The bank says that since roughly this time last year it had been concerned about the risk to management guidance and the lack of a turn in the freight cycle. "Today's results allow us to pivot our view as guidance has been de-risked and re-based down to more achievable levels." Full-year EBIT guidance is in line with the bank's estimates and the medium-term guidance now doesn't try to predict economic cycles, Chu says. The bank upgrades Deutsche Post to buy from hold and raises its target price to 50 euros from 36 euros. Shares fall 2.4% to 43.22 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0825 GMT - Deutsche Post's investment case has been strengthened by management actions to contain costs and boost growth, JPMorgan analysts write. The company delivered strong fourth-quarter EBIT, derisked forward guidance by targeting an EBIT level it is confident in reaching despite macro uncertainties, and outlined a cost-improvement plan. "While share price performance year to date has been strong, partly driven by sentiment around German equities, we think the fact that management is demonstrating it is taking action to ensure earnings growth resumes, will help the investment case." JPMorgan lifts its price target on the stock to 50 euros from 42.50 euros and reiterates an overweight rating. Shares fall 1.8% to 43.48 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - Ninebot is poised to strengthen its dominance in the high-end intelligent electric two-wheeler market, Daiwa Capital Markets analysts say in a research report. Initiating coverage with a buy rating and a target price of CNY70.00, the brokerage projects the company's market share in China's high-end segment will reach around 36% by 2027. Analysts cite the company's leadership in intelligent technology, strong brand awareness among young consumers and ongoing nationwide expansion of its distribution network. Daiwa forecasts EPS CAGR of 37% for Ninebot over 2025-2027. Shares closed 2.1% higher at CNY60.39. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0248 GMT - Airports of Thailand faces greater hit to FY 2025 earnings from the delay in concession payments, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report. The Thai company this week clarified that King Power Duty Free's concession payment will be postponed from September 2024 to April 2025 and King Power Selection's concession payment will be delayed from September 2024 to February 2025. Management at its 1Q FY 2025 analysts' meeting said that KPD and KPS had requested to delay concession payments from August 2024 to February 2025. These changes could have an estimated THB2.1 billion negative impact on AOT's FY 2025 concession revenue, the analysts say. The brokerage cuts the stock's target price to THB49.00 from THB52.00 with unchanged hold rating. Shares closed at THB40.50 on Thursday. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0019 GMT - Napier Port measures up well against its chief listed rival in New Zealand for stock pickers, according to Forsyth Barr. "Napier Port is trading at a 50% discount to its larger sector peer, Port of Tauranga, which presents an attractive entry opportunity, not least given a robust medium-term earnings growth outlook," analyst Andy Bowley says. Napier Port this week said it's tracking toward the top end of its FY 2025 Ebitda guidance range. It also reiterated a key goal for return on capital. Napier Port is down 2.2% today, amid broad-based weakness among NZ stocks. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1642 GMT - America's Car-Mart sold more cars in its recent quarter than it did a year earlier, despite operating in what CEO Doug Campbell says on a call with analysts is an uncertain market, in which used car affordability is a major concern among consumers. "The work that we've done as a company, by centralizing certain functions, finding new sources of inventory and tightening underwriting standards, will help us navigate the environment more effectively," he says. The company feels good heading into the seasonally-strong spring selling season, Campbell says, having added inventory and fine tuned its marketing strategy, while continuing to pursue opportunities to improve deal structures. Shares rise 18% following a better-than-expected F3Q. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Swiss Equities Ends Week in Green; Mikron Shares Climb
data.description:
The Swiss Market Index recovered on Friday, closing 0.36%, amid another earnings-heavy day and a slew of economic data prints.

Switzerland's foreign currency reserves declined to 735.37 billion francs in February from 736.44 billion francs in January, according to data from the Swiss National Bank.

In the euro area, seasonally adjusted gross domestic product rose 0.2% in the fourth quarter of 2024 after a 0.4% increase in the previous quarter, Eurostat's third estimate showed. On the labor front, the number of employed individuals in the region ticked up 0.1% in the fourth quarter of 2024, following a 0.2% rise in the prior three-month period.

Across the pond in the US, total nonfarm payroll employment rose by 151,000 in February, up from the revised 125,000 in January and lower than market expectations of 160,000. The unemployment rate edged up to 4.1% from 4%.

"The US economy added 151k jobs in February, but with DOGE's influence increasingly being felt on the economy the risk is that we start to see renewed softness in the months ahead," ING said. "With tariffs also likely to result in some price rises, putting a squeeze on spending power, we continue to look for rate cuts to resume from the third quarter."

In corporate news, Mikron Holding shares climbed 8.11% as it booked a year-over-year increase in 2024 net sales to 374.1 million francs from 370.2 million francs, with a "healthy" order backlog. Looking ahead to 2025, the Swiss microelectronics manufacturer expects overall net sales to remain stable, with an operating profit margin similar to the 8.5% recorded in 2024.

Meanwhile, Flughafen Zürich reported record financial results in 2024, with consolidated result rising to 326.7 million francs from the year-ago 304.2 million francs, marking the highest in the company's history. For 2025, the Swiss airport operator forecasts an estimated 32 million passengers to fly through the airport, reaching a new record. However, it also expects consolidated profit in 2025 to be lower than the year before amid depreciation and interest expenses. The stock was down 1.57% at closing.

data.title: Sugar Steady Amid Supply Concerns
data.description:
Sugar futures hovered around 18.4 cents per pound, stabilizing after a sharp decline to their lowest level since January 23rd, as concerns over tighter global supplies for the 2024/25 season countered weak demand and abundant stockpiles.

The International Sugar Organization raised its global sugar deficit forecast to 4.88 MMT from 2.51 MMT, citing lower-than-expected Indian output, while adverse weather in Brazil fueled supply concerns.

However, bearish factors persist, with Brazil’s 2025/26 sugar output projected at a record 43.6 MMT, amid sugar production being more profitable than ethanol.

Large sugar deliveries by traders like Wilmar International reflect weak demand, while a subdued Brazilian real continues to incentivize exports, exacerbating oversupply.

Further downward pressure stemmed from the imminent expiration of the front-month contract.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1215 ET - Base metal prices fall but are set to finish the week higher, with LME three-month copper down 0.9% at $9,601.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.1% at $2,700 a ton. President Trump's tariff threats have driven New York-traded copper prices up significantly compared to LME prices, Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen says in a note. Earlier this week, Trump repeated his intention to tariff copper, driving prices of U.S. futures sharply higher. The copper market may have reacted prematurely, as the investigation into the metal under Section 232 of the Trade Act normally takes months--but the 25% tariff threat was clearly higher than the market expected, and U.S. traders are now scrambling to price in the correct level, Hansen says. The U.S. copper market will likely exhibit greater volatility, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1159 ET - CME live cattle futures return to climbing after a quick breather through yesterday's trading session. Most-active futures are up 1.7% in morning trade, with the delays made to the implementation of tariffs of Mexico and Canada seen as a positive for U.S. beef, as the U.S. is currently a net importer of cattle — getting most of their imports from Mexico. "Ag market price action on Thursday was primarily a function of the Trump Administration reversion to the USMCA... this supported ag markets--including cattle and feeder cattle," says AgResource in a note. Lean hogs are up 0.4%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1117 ET - News of the partial tariff pullback on Mexico and Canada is a relief for farmers heading into spring planting, as tariffs on key fertilizer ingredient potash are also being negotiated lower. Canada is the world's largest producer and exporter of potash. "The decisions, which are in effect until April 2, are welcome news for American farmers," says Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing in a note. Most-active corn rises 0.5%, soybeans inch up 0.1%, and wheat slides 0.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1018 ET - Foreign-exchange traders have yet to come to terms that the heightened uncertainty over the Trump administration's tariffs "is still really, really bad for Canada," says Brent Donnelly, head of forex-research firm Spectra Markets. He says upcoming Canadian economic indicators are going to take on paramount importance, as they will provide evidence about how tariff uncertainty is weighing on activity. Some analysts say the lackluster Canada jobs report is a sign of a pullback in hiring. Donnelly says a downturn in Canadian data "will lead to massive CAD selling," adding "the market loves the theme but hasn't been able to hold on because of the unexpected reaction in USDCAD." CAD largely held steady this week relative to USD. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1002 ET - Strong bearish sentiment across oil markets is expected to be short-lived, Rystad Energy's global head of commodity markets Mukesh Sahdev says. In afternoon trade, Brent crude and WTI both rise 2.1% to $70.93 and $67.76 a barrel, respectively, buoyed by Russia's Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak reportedly saying OPEC+ could reverse its output hike plans after April in case of market imbalances. "While the delay in tariffs has provided a brief sigh of relief, the market is still walking a tightrope between policy uncertainty and oversupply concerns," Sahdev says. However, "we project the drop in prices will be temporary and OPEC+ will take corrective measures as the crude time spreads fall below $0.50 per barrel and the market flirts with contango." Contango occurs when the price of a commodity for future delivery is higher than the spot price, indicating traders expect prices to rise. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0952 ET - The cost for a gallon of orange juice found a new all-time high in the past month, according to the latest data from Nielsen. The firm says that the average price for the four weeks ended Feb. 22 was $10.82/gal. That marks the third consecutive month that retail orange juice prices set a new high. This year alone, OJ prices have jumped 8% — and they're up nearly 14% from this time last year. Supporting prices is Florida's small crop, and tariff actions by President Trump complicate the supply chain for juice oranges from major producer Mexico. FCOJ futures trading on the ICE have been under pressure despite these factors, recently falling under $3 a pound for the first time since January of last year. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0935 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are returning some of the week's gains after the market rallied in the face of a milder weather outlook, rising output, and easing LNG demand, capped by a smaller-than-expected inventory withdrawal reported by the EIA. While the long-term outlook remains bullish and upside price risk remains, receding momentum and soft fundamentals increase the likelihood of a near-term price retreat, Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. The Nymex front month is off 3.3% at $4.161/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0855 ET - Gold futures rise as gold exchange-traded funds report inflows in February. Futures are up 0.15% at $2,930.90 a troy ounce. Gold ETFs recorded strong inflows of 100 metric tons in February, the third consecutive month of inflows, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. This ETF demand helped propel gold to a record high of $2,974.0 an ounce in late February, Commerzbank says. The inflows were mainly concentrated in the U.S., where the largest inflow in a single month since July 2020, was recorded. European ETFs, on the other hand, only recorded a small increase in holdings. China saw ETF inflows of around 20 tons, or an increase of around 19%--indicating a growing interest in gold ETFs among Chinese investors. Until now, most Chinese investment demand was dominated by gold bars and coins, Commerzbank adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0845 ET - Oil futures attempt a recovery after President Trump postpones import tariffs on a range of goods from Mexico and Canada for another month, easing one of the bearish factors behind the recent selloff that include OPEC+ plans to start unwinding cuts next month. "While the delay in tariffs has provided a brief sigh of relief, the market is still walking a tightrope between policy uncertainty and oversupply concerns," Rystad Energy's Mukesh Sahdev says in a note. "The tariff threats sent shockwaves through markets, not just because of potential trade disruptions, but also due to fears of retaliatory moves that could ripple through energy supply chains." WTI is up 1.5% at $67.34 a barrel, and Brent is up 1.5% at $70.52 a barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0716 ET - Cleveland-Cliffs is launching a company-wide "Buy American" incentive for its nearly 30,000 employees. During calendar year 2025, any Cleveland-Cliffs employee who buys or leases a new American-built vehicle with substantial Cliffs' steel content will receive a $1,000 cash bonus in connection with the purchase. Vehicles produced in a plant outside of the U.S., or that use a significant amount of imported flat-rolled steel, will not qualify for the incentive. "We hope that other companies will follow suit and do their part to incentivize consumption of American-made products," says Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs' chairman and CEO. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0627 ET - Base metal prices fall, but remain up more than 2% on week. LME three-month copper is down 0.1% at $9,679 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum is down 0.2% at $2,698 a ton. The rally over the week reflects a weaker U.S. dollar, President Trump's copper tariff comments and renewed China growth optimism, SP Angel analysts say in a note. Chinese Premier Li Qiang said China has set an economic growth goal of around 5% for 2025 despite trade war volatility, and the country has continued to hint at further stimulus support, SP Angel writes. This has boosted expectations for industrial metal demand going forward, while the weaker greenback makes it cheaper for international buyers to purchase dollar-denominated commodities. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0508 ET - Palm oil ended higher, tracking soybean oil's overnight gains on the Chicago Board of Trade. Market sentiment improved following the U.S. decision to temporarily exempt tariffs on some imports from Canada and Mexico, Kenanga Futures said in a note. In addition, India's palm oil imports saw a 36% on-month surge in February, recovering from a significant dip in January, Phillip Nova said, citing dealer estimates. Investors await the official February supply and demand data by the Malaysian Palm Oil Board due Monday. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery ended 147 ringgit higher at 4,627 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Base Metal Prices Set to End Week Higher on Tariff Worries — Market Talk
data.description: Base metal prices fall but are set to finish the week higher, with LME three-month copper down 0.9% at $9,601.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.1% at $2,700 a ton. President Trump's tariff threats have driven New York-traded copper prices up significantly compared to LME prices, Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen says in a note. Earlier this week, Trump repeated his intention to tariff copper, driving prices of U.S. futures sharply higher. The copper market may have reacted prematurely, as the investigation into the metal under Section 232 of the Trade Act normally takes months--but the 25% tariff threat was clearly higher than the market expected, and U.S. traders are now scrambling to price in the correct level, Hansen says. The U.S. copper market will likely exhibit greater volatility, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.

data.title: TSX Down 125 Points at Midday
data.description:
The Toronto Stock Exchange is down 125 points at midday, with miners (-3%) and info tech (-2%), the biggest decliners.

Telecom (+2%) and energy (+1.8%) are the biggest gainers.

Oil prices rose following reports Russia's oil minister said OPEC+ may pause the return of 2.2-million barrels of daily production cuts to the market following the initial April tranche. Gold prices edged higher early on Friday as the dollar fell to a four-month low after the U.S. reported a smaller than expected rise in employment last month.

But natural gas prices fell for a second day as milder spring weather lowers heating demand while a day-prior inventory report showed storage fell less than expected last week.

Bloomberg has noted today that U.S. President Donald Trump said tariffs on Mexico and Canada could go higher than a 25% rate imposed earlier this week, injecting further uncertainty into trade policies that have rattled markets and left businesses on edge. Trump's comments came in an interview that aired Friday, a day after he paused those tariffs until April 2 for Mexican and Canadian goods covered by the USMCA trade agreement he struck in his first term — a move that offered a brief reprieve for two major US trading partners.

Desjardins noted that as businesses awaited news on tariffs, hiring in the Canadian economy stalled in February. The Canadian economy only added 1K jobs over the month, well below expectations for a 20K increase. That comes after three months of strong job gains in which the economy added 211K new jobs. The unemployment rate remained unchanged at 6.6% largely due to a two tick drop in the participation rate. Desjardins said the slowdown in hiring even before tariffs were implemented gives the Bank of Canada an easy pass to cut interest rates another 25 basis points next week. Desjardins noted markets are pricing almost 90% odds that the central bank eases again. Rates were falling across the Government of Canada yield curve following the weak jobs numbers after yesterday's selloff.

Elsewhere, a Macquarie Strategist has weighed in on why President Trump dropped the punitive tariffs on Canada and Mexico.

Thierry Wizman, Global FX & Rates Strategist at Macquarie, noted yesterday's was the second (partial) reprieve from punitive 25% tariffs for Canada and Mexico. He said Macquarie can think of a few reasons why it was done, including: the first is domestic politics; the second is legal considerations; the third is domestic U.S. price inflation; and the fourth is concessions.

Finally, Wizman said, another motivator for delaying tariffs may have been Canada's changing political and electoral dynamics. Trump would prefer to have a Conservative majority-led government in Canada, rather than a Liberal-led government, as has been the case since 2015. "And Trump was probably informed — and wised-up — to the premise that his tariffs on Canada should have waited until after Canada's election, if they came at all, because they had so far only served to help install his ideological rivals into power. Trying to resuscitate the Conservatives' chances may have been a reason for letting Canada off the hook on tariffs."

IP Group plc ("IP Group" or "the Group"), today announces that Numis Securities Limited (which is trading for these purposes as Deutsche Numis) ("Deutsche Numis"), purchased on behalf of IP Group the number of ordinary shares of 2 pence each on the London Stock Exchange as set out in the table below (the Repurchased Shares) pursuant to the IP Group share buyback programme, an extension to which was announced on 09 January 2025 (the Buyback Programme).

Date of purchase:

07/03/2025

Number of ordinary shares purchased:

440,000

Highest price paid per ordinary share (GBp):

38.60

Lowest price paid per ordinary share (GBp): 

37.65

38.2837

Any Ordinary Shares purchased under the Buyback Programme will be cancelled in order to decrease the number of Ordinary Shares in issue. Accordingly, following the above transaction and subsequent cancellation of the Repurchased Shares, the Group has 958,242,125 ordinary shares in issue with voting rights. This number may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, IP Group under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules. data.title: Toronto Stocks Slide; MDA Space Rises on Tariff Resilience, Growth Targets
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto stocks were lower Friday, mid-trading, and on track for a weekly decline after a tumultuous few days of U.S. tariff developments. Market performance was mixed in the session, with gains in tech, energy and communications partly offset by losses in industrial services, materials and tech-services stocks.

On the broader economic front, Canada's labor market effectively stalled last month as hiring flatlined, leaving the jobless rate steady at 6.6%.

Meanwhile, Canada's industrial capacity utilization rate rose 0.4% to 79.8% during the final quarter of 2024, supported by a recovery in the construction industry as building activity has picked up.

At midday, Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index fell 0.3% to 24515.75 and blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 declined 0.3% to 1477.02.

Shares of MDA Space rose by 11% to 25.25 Canadian dollars ($17.66) after executives at the Brampton, Ontario, aerospace-technology company said MDA can manage a U.S.-imposed tariff environment, and plotted a course for better-than-expected growth this year on the back of strong demand.

Other market movers:

Algonquin Power & Utilities shares fell 5.3% to C$6.47 after the company reported a loss and lower revenue in the fourth quarter as the company continues its transition to a pure-play utility company.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: BioNexus Gene Partners With ML Tech for Treasury Strategy
data.description:
BioNexus Gene Lab said it is partnering with ML Tech to optimize its Ethereum-focused treasury strategy.

The company said it plans to leverage ML Tech's digital asset trading infrastructure to grow its Ethereum holdings. ML Tech is a wealth management platform for digital assets.

Shares of the company were up over 3% in recent trading.

data.title: Market Chatter: Elliott Management Takes $727.2 Million Short Position in TotalEnergies
data.description:
Hedge fund Elliott Management has taken a 670 million euro ($727.2 million) short position in French energy major TotalEnergies , The Financial Times reported Friday, citing regulatory filings.

Neither TotalEnergies nor Elliott Management immediately responded to MT Newswires' requests for comment.

Market Purchase by the Company of its own Shares

Notification is given that, pursuant to the authority granted at the Annual General Meeting of the Company held on 2 October 2024 to make market purchases of the Company's own ordinary shares up to a maximum of 14.99%, a market purchase of 30,000 ordinary shares in the capital of the Company was made on 7 March 2025 at a price of 782.6667p per share. The purchased shares will be held in Treasury. 

Following the above purchase, the Company's issued ordinary share capital continues to be 74,385,131 ordinary shares of 25p each, of which 1,482,770 shares are held in Treasury and have no voting rights.

Holders of the Company's ordinary shares are entitled to one vote for every share held. As Treasury shares have no voting rights, the total number of voting rights is 72,902,361. There are also 4,257 preference stock units in issue, which do not carry voting rights.
